title: Allocation and deallocation

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Before malloc(count * sizeof *items), code checks count against SIZE_MAX / sizeof *items and verifies the returned pointer before use. Your goal is to implement a dynamically sized array without leak, double-free or lost-pointer errors. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic; Structures; Object lifetime and ownership; Conversions and numeric boundaries. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Dynamic allocation requests a byte region with suitable alignment, checks failure, establishes objects through valid access, and pairs each owned allocation with exactly one eventual free. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Size multiplication can overflow, realloc can move or fail while preserving the old block, zero-size requests are special, and allocation does not initialize ordinary bytes. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: malloc initializes every allocated element to zero and realloc failure frees the original block automatically.

includes: Use malloc, calloc, realloc and free with overflow and failure checks.
Implement a dynamically sized array without leak, double-free or lost-pointer errors.
excludes: Size multiplication can overflow, realloc can move or fail while preserving the old block, zero-size requests are special, and allocation does not initialize ordinary bytes.

outcomes: Implement a dynamically sized array without leak, double-free or lost-pointer errors.

examples.title: Allocation and deallocation: defined case
examples.situation: Before malloc(count * sizeof *items), code checks count against SIZE_MAX / sizeof *items and verifies the returned pointer before use.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Before malloc(count * sizeof *items), code checks count against SIZE_MAX / sizeof *items and verifies the returned pointer before use.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: malloc returns uninitialized storage and realloc failure leaves the original block allocated; check sizes and results before changing the owning pointer.
examples.result: The defined result follows because malloc returns uninitialized storage and realloc failure leaves the original block allocated; check sizes and results before changing the owning pointer.

examples.title: Allocation and deallocation: boundary case
examples.situation: A temporary pointer receives realloc so failure does not overwrite and leak the still-valid original allocation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A temporary pointer receives realloc so failure does not overwrite and leak the still-valid original allocation.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Size multiplication can overflow, realloc can move or fail while preserving the old block, zero-size requests are special, and allocation does not initialize ordinary bytes.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: malloc initializes every allocated element to zero and realloc failure frees the original block automatically.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement a dynamically sized array without leak, double-free or lost-pointer errors.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: malloc initializes every allocated element to zero and realloc failure frees the original block automatically.
misconceptions.correction: malloc returns uninitialized storage and realloc failure leaves the original block allocated; check sizes and results before changing the owning pointer.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: malloc initializes every allocated element to zero and realloc failure frees the original block automatically.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Allocation and deallocation: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Size multiplication can overflow, realloc can move or fail while preserving the old block, zero-size requests are special, and allocation does not initialize ordinary bytes.
